hook.text: Galaxies didn't always exist. The first ones formed just a few hundred million years after the Big Bang.

buildup.text: Dark matter clumped first, creating gravitational wells. Gas fell in, ignited stars, and galaxies were born.

discovery.text: JWST found galaxies from just 300 million years after the Big Bang—far earlier than anyone expected.

twist.text: These early galaxies were surprisingly bright and massive. Current models struggle to explain how they formed so fast.

climax.text: Understanding the first galaxies may require rethinking how dark matter and gas interacted in the early cosmos.

punchline.text: Galaxies appeared shockingly early. The universe built fast.

quiz.question: What surprised astronomers about the earliest galaxies found by JWST?
options: They were far more massive and bright than models predicted for that early era
They contained no stars at all—only dark matter
They were much smaller and dimmer than expected
